segments.s598-g65: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' placed at the clause start. داستانی is an indefinite noun ('a tale') that will be described; شگفت is an adjective ('wondrous/astonishing') modifying داستانی and appears after it, as in Persian adjective‑after‑noun order. بگویم is 1st person singular present‑subjunctive (classical Persian usage) functioning like a future or narrative 'I will tell' — its placement between the noun and adjective is a poetic inversion but grammatically links the speaker to the forthcoming tale.
segments.s598-g66: کزان is a relative/compound connector meaning 'from/of which' (classical compound of که + از آن) introducing a relative clause. برتر اندازه is a noun phrase 'greater/higher measure' (برتر qualifies اندازه; here اندازه means 'measure/extent'). نتوان گرفتن is a negative modal construction: نتوان (cannot) + گرفت (past form of گرفتن used impersonally/passive) yields 'cannot be taken' or 'cannot be measured', so the whole clause means 'from which no greater measure can be taken' (i.e., incomparable or beyond measure).
segments.s598-g1: چو is an archaic/poetic conjunction meaning “when” introducing a temporal clause. The sequence 'بهرام و خسرو به هامون شدند' places the actors (Bahrām and Khosrow) before the verb شدند (past plural of شدن), here meaning “arrived/turned up/came” to the location هامون (the plain). The preposition به precedes the place (هامون).
segments.s598-g2: بر شیر is a prepositional phrase meaning “on/upon a lion” (بر = on). With introduces the manner/companionship phrase 'با دل پر از خون' where پر از is the standard Persian pattern “full of X” (دل = heart). The final شدند repeats the past plural verb, yielding a coordinated action: they mounted and they had hearts full of blood.
segments.s598-g3: چو introduces a new temporal clause “when Khosrow saw…”. بدید is the past tense “saw.” آن دو شیر ژیان is a noun phrase: آن دو = those/two, شیر ژیان = “living/virile lions” (ژیان is an epithet related to life or vigor) modifying شیر.
segments.s598-g4: نهاده is a past participle/verb meaning “placed/put” and here functions as a verblike predicate describing the state of an object. یکی افسر = “one helmet.” اندر میان is a fixed locative phrase meaning “in the middle / in the midst.” Word order places the participle before its object and locative complement.
segments.s598-g5: بدان موبدان گفت = به + آن موبدان “to those mobeds” (an older combination: به/بدان used to mark the indirect object) followed by گفت “(he) said.” تاج از نخست is a comparative turn: از in comparisons functions like “than”/“from” and نخست = “the first/foremost.” The clause therefore sets up a comparison about the crown.
segments.s598-g6: مر is an archaic dative pronoun meaning “to me.” آن را is a topical/accusative demonstrative phrase “that (thing)” with را marking the definite object. سزاتر is the comparative form of سزا meaning “more deserving / more fitting.” که introduces the subordinate clause explaining the reason; شاهی بجست is a compact past clause “(he) sought kingship” (بجست = past of جستن, here “sought”).
segments.s598-g7: و دیگر که introduces an additional reason: دیگر here means “another / moreover,” and که introduces the content clause. من پیرم و او جوان is a simple coordinated clause with the copular adjective forms پیرم (I am old) and جوان (he is young); Persian often omits the explicit copula in such adjectival predicates.
segments.s598-g8: به چنگال = a prepositional phrase “into / in the claws of.” شیر ژیان again uses ژیان as an epithet for شیر (“the living/ferocious lion”). ناتوان is a predicative adjective meaning “incapable / powerless”; the construction implies that the (older) speaker is unable in the lion’s grasp (no explicit copula needed).
segments.s598-g49: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/nobles.' برو is an adverbial/verb phrase here meaning 'went forth' (a poetic auxiliary action) placed after the subject. افشاندند is the main verb in past tense, 3rd person plural 'they scattered/sprinkled,' so the sequence reads literally 'the great ones went forth and scattered jewels.' Word order (subject + adverbial verb + main verb) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated poetic style.
segments.s598-g50: بران is a locative adverb 'upon that/thereon,' linking this clause to the previous action. تاج نو is a noun + adjective 'new crown.' آفرین here is the interjection/acclamation 'Afarin!' (not the verb 'to create'), and خواندند (past, 3rd pl.) means 'they cried/sang/called out' — together forming 'they cried "Afarin" upon that new crown.'
segments.s598-g51: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گیتی is 'the world.' برآمد is an intransitive past verb 'arose/issued forth.' سراسر ('throughout') modifies خروش ('cry/uproar'), so the noun phrase 'سراسر خروش' means 'a cry that filled everything.' The whole segment literally: 'From the world arose a cry throughout.'
segments.s598-g52: در introduces a temporal/location phrase 'in' or 'during.' آذر here names the month or period (proper name) rather than the element 'fire.' بد is an adjective 'ill/evil/ill-omened' qualifying that month. این جشن روز سروش is a genitive/attributive sequence 'this festival-day of Soroush' (سروش = the angel/Soroush). The phrase gives the time/occasion: 'in ill-omened Azar, (on) this festival-day of Soroush.'
segments.s598-g53: برآمد is 'arose' (past, 3rd sing.) and stands first in a poetic inversion. یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' qualifying ابر 'cloud' (i.e. 'a cloud arose'). و 'and' links the clauses; شد is past of 'become' and takes تیره‌ماه as its predicate: 'and the moon became dark.'
segments.s598-g54: همی is a frequent archaic/poetic particle indicating continuation or immediacy ('kept on' / 'then/indeed'); here it marks the ongoing action. تیر (literally 'arrows') is used metaphorically as the things that 'rained' (بارید, past 'fell/rained'). ز introduces the source 'from the black cloud' (ابر سیاه). The verb بارید is 3rd-person past, so the clause reads 'arrows rained from a black cloud.'
segments.s598-g55: The triple negation pattern نه ... و نه ... و نه ... is a conventional poetic listing meaning 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'—here applied to دریا 'sea', دشت 'plain', and راغ (an abstract noun here 'longing/desire'). پدید (pādid) is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'appears' or 'is manifest.' Literally: 'Neither sea appears, nor plain, nor longing.'
segments.s598-g56: نبینم is first-person singular present negative 'I do not see.' همی here emphasizes or gives a continuous sense ('indeed' / 'constantly'). در هوا 'in the air' gives the location; پر زاغ is a compound meaning 'full of rooks' (پر = full (of), زاغ = rook/crow). Altogether: 'I do not see ...; indeed I see the air full of rooks.'
segments.s598-g57: This clause presents an ongoing action: 'حواصل' (harvests, yields) is the direct object and 'هوا' (the air/wind) is the agent performing 'فشاند' (scatters/sprinkles). Word order is V‑O‑S/SVO flexible in classical Persian; here the verb 'فشاند' precedes its agent for poetic effect. 'هر زمان' is an adverbial phrase (‘at every moment’), with 'هر' modifying 'زمان'.
segments.s598-g58: 'چه سازد همی زین بلند آسمان' is a rhetorical question: 'چه سازد' = 'what can (it) do / what does (it) make/do'. 'همی' is an archaic/poetic durative adverb meaning 'always/still' that emphasizes continuality. 'زین' here is the contracted classical form of 'زِ این / از این' (= 'from/by this'), so it functions as a prepositional phrase with 'بلند آسمان' = 'the lofty sky'; since that usage of 'زین' is not listed among the provided senses, it is marked accordingly.
segments.s598-g59: 'نماندم' is the past tense (first person or impersonal) of 'ماندن' used here in an impersonal/indefinite sense: 'there remained not' / 'nothing was left.' The following items are listed as absent: 'نمکسود' (salt‑cured food), then 'هیزم' (firewood), then 'جو' (barley). The coordinating 'و' simply links list items; the negative sense covers all listed nouns.
segments.s598-g60: 'نه چیزی پدیدست' = 'nothing appears / nothing is to be found' — 'پدیدست' is a present/stative verb meaning 'is manifest/appears.' 'تا' here is temporal/subordinating meaning 'until,' implying nothing is found until the arrival/appearance of the person described by 'جودرو' (an epithet meaning 'munificent' or 'of generous appearance').
segments.s598-g61: The opening adverbial 'بدین' (classical fused form meaning 'by this' / 'because of this') introduces cause: 'بدین تیرگی روز و بیم خراج' = 'by this daytime gloom and the fear of tax/tribute.' In the tokenization shown, 'ب' and 'دین' are split, but the whole functions as 'by this'; 'تیرگی' = 'darkness/gloom' modifies 'روز' = 'day', and 'بیم' = 'fear' modifies 'خراج' = 'tribute/tax'.
segments.s598-g62: 'زمین گشته از برف' uses 'گشته' (from 'گشتن') as 'has become / is turned' (perfective/resultative), so 'the earth has become from snow...' The simile follows: 'چون کوه عاج' = 'like a mountain of ivory,' where 'چون' = 'like/as', 'کوه' = 'mountain', and 'عاج' = 'ivory' (image produced by accumulated snow).
segments.s598-g63: 'همه کارها را' is the definite object/topic 'all affairs' marked by 'را'. 'سراندر نشیب' is a compact poetic expression meaning 'cast into decline / in a low/descent state' — 'سراندر' (within/over) + 'نشیب' (descent/decline). Together they state that everything has fallen into hardship or decline.
segments.s598-g64: 'مگر' introduces the exception ('except/unless'). 'دست گیرد' is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to take in hand / to assume control' — 'دست' = 'hand (i.e., power/control)' and 'گیرد' = 'takes/seizes'. The proper name follows: 'حسین قتیب' (Husayn Qetīb), so the clause means all is in decline except when Husayn Qetīb should take charge.
segments.s598-g9: بران بد here functions as a comment clause meaning 'it is bad/ill for that' (literally 'upon that bad'), introducing the complaint; که + present-form کند gives a subordinate clause ('that he should ...'). کند is the classical present/subjunctive/present-tense form used here as the verbal nucleus: 'that he takes/may take the initiative.'
segments.s598-g10: به here marks the manner or instrument ('with/using'); the two nouns برنایی and تن‌درستی name qualities ('youthfulness' and 'bodily robustness') that characterize how he acts. کند repeats the verbal idea from the previous clause, so the whole phrase means 'he acts with youthful vigor and bodily soundness.'
segments.s598-g11: بدو گفت is the common classical word order 'to him said' (i.e., 'said to him'), with گفت as the past tense verb 'said' and بهرام as the nominative/subject preceding the predicate 'کری رواست' (literally 'deafness/performance of deafness is permitted'). ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s598-g12: نهانی نداریم is a negated present clause 'we have no secret' (نداریم = 'we do not have'). گفتار راست ('speech true') is a noun phrase 'a straight/true speech' or 'plain speaking'—here it functions as what there is no secrecy about and implies an instruction to speak plainly.
segments.s598-g13: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' and introduces the person; گاوسر is a proper name attached to that indefinite. گرزه 'mace' is the object, and برگرفت is the past tense 'took up/raised' (بر-گرفتن), describing the single swift action.
segments.s598-g14: جهانی ('the whole/worldly [world]') is subject-like here and بدو ('at/toward him') marks the target of astonishment; مانده is the perfect/periphrastic 'remained/was left' (i.e. 'stood'), and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment'—together: 'the whole world stood amazed at him.'
segments.s598-g15: بدو گفت موبد is 'the mobed (Zoroastrian priest) said to him' (again the common 'to him said' order). که‌ای is a vocative sequence (که + ای) functioning as an address marker 'O', followed by پادشا 'king'—so the priest is directly addressing the king.
segments.s598-g16: A string of epithets in apposition to the addressee: خردمند 'wise', بادانش here functions as 'learned/knowledgeable' (classical orthography of با دانش), and پارسا 'pious'. The conjunctions و simply link the adjectives; the whole is an honorific description of the king.
segments.s598-g25: هم‌آورد here is an archaic/poetic predicate meaning 'be equal to' or 'be a match for.' The phrase هم‌آورد این نره شیران is a nominal phrase 'the match of these male lions,' and the final منم ('I am') places the speaker as that match — subject identity expressed after the descriptive phrase, a common poetic word order.
segments.s598-g26: خریدار is used metaphorically: 'one who takes/accepts' rather than literally 'buyer.' جنگ دلیران is a genitive/construct: 'the battle of the brave (men).' The sentence ends again with منم, repeating the identification 'I am' — i.e. 'I am the one who takes this battle.'
segments.s598-g27: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him' placed before the verb. گفت is the past speech verb ('said') and موبد is the subject. به یزدان پناه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/under God's refuge' (an exhortation/imperative content that follows the speech verb).
segments.s598-g28: چو رفتی introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when you go' (چو = 'when', رفتی = 2nd-person past/perfect or verbal form used with subjunctive/imperative context). دلت را is the object with possessive suffix (دل + ت = 'your heart') plus object marker را. بشوی here is a subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'wash/cleanse' (addressing the second person); از گناه is a prepositional phrase 'from/of sin' explaining what to be cleansed from.
segments.s598-g29: چنان کرد is the main clause 'he did thus/so' (چنان = 'thus', کرد = past 'did'). کو is an archaic/poetic relative/connector meaning roughly 'who/that/which' linking to the following reported speech; گفت here functions as 'had said' and بهرامشاه names the speaker. The clause therefore means 'he did as Bahram-shah had said.'
segments.s598-g30: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + ـش). پاک شد is a simple past passive/first-resultative 'became pure/was cleansed.' توبه کرد is another past clause 'he made repentance/repented.' از گناه ('of/from sin') marks the object of the repentance. The two clauses are coordinated, describing internal change and the act of repenting.
segments.s598-g31: همی رفت uses همی as a habitual/continuous marker with the past رفت to mean 'he kept going' or 'he was going.' با گرزه گاوروی is an instrumental/genitive sequence: با + گرزه = 'with a mace' and گاوروی qualifies گرزه (the cowherd's mace or 'a mace like a cowherd's'), so the whole phrase says he moved on armed with that rustic cudgel.
segments.s598-g32: چو دیدند is a temporal clause 'when they saw' (چو = 'when', دیدند = 3rd-plural past 'they saw'). شیران is the subject 'the lions' and پرخاشجوی is a postpositive adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' qualifying them. The object of دیدند (what they saw) is left implicit in the line.
segments.s598-g33: Literal order: 'یکی زود' = 'one (of them) quickly' (numeral/pronoun + adverb). 'زنجیر بگسست' uses the past tense بگسست (begasast) meaning 'broke' (3rd singular); the verb follows its subject (زنجیر). The conjunction و simply links to the next nominal بند, which names the agent or the thing continuing the action in the next clause.
segments.s598-g34: بیامد is the past narrative 'came' (3rd singular). The preposition بر governs شهریار بلند ('the lofty king'); the adjective بلند follows and qualifies شهریار in typical poetic order. Together this clause means '(he) came upon/at the lofty king.'
segments.s598-g35: بزد is the simple past 'he struck'. The prepositional phrase بر سرش = 'upon his head' (بر + noun + enclitic possessive ش). The instrument follows the verb: گرز بهرام (the mace associated with Bahram); گرد here is an adjectival/epithet-like word attached after the instrument (poetic ordering).
segments.s598-g36: ز is the poetic 'from'. چشمش = 'his eye' (noun + possessive enclitic). همی is a classical narrative particle marking a continuous/immediate action ('then/at once' or 'continually' in older usage). روشنایی is the object 'light' and ببرد is a present/subjunctive form used in epic narration with the force of a past action—here 'took away' or 'the light left'.
segments.s598-g37: بر دیگر آمد = 'another came upon' (بر + pronoun/adverb دیگر + آمد 'came'); the sequence then continues with بزد 'he struck', followed by the prepositional phrase بر سرش 'on his head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش). The subject is again implicit (the attacker).
segments.s598-g38: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal idea: فرو (downward) + ریخت (poured/spilled) = 'poured down' or 'flowed down'. از دیده خون = 'blood from the eye' (از = from). The final از برش reads 'and from his بر (cut/wound)' where -ش is the 3rd-person possessive enclitic; برش therefore means 'his wound/cut' (a wound from the blow).
segments.s598-g39: جهاندار is the subject meaning 'sovereign' or 'world-holder'. بنشست is past 'sat down'. بر تخت عاج is the locative phrase 'on the ivory throne' (preposition بر + تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory' as the descriptive word after the noun).
segments.s598-g40: به سر بر نهاد is a classical verbal cluster: به سر = 'on (his) head' (به as directional/locative), بر نهاد = 'placed on' (نهاد past of نهادن 'to place', with بر as part of the verbal phrase). آن دلفروز تاج = 'that heart-illuminating crown' (آن demonstrative + epithet دلفروز + noun تاج), i.e. he placed that crown upon his head.
segments.s598-g17: همی here is an emphatic/illustrative particle ('this very' / 'indeed') placed before the noun phrase. جنگ شیران is a noun phrase 'the battle/war of lions' where شیران (literally 'lions') is used metaphorically for brave fighters. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that', and فرمایدت is the past-tense verb 'you commanded/ordered' (فرمود + enclitic -ت), so the whole segment = 'that battle of lions which you commanded'.
segments.s598-g18: جز از ... چه افزایدت is a concessive/interrogative phrase: جز از = 'apart from / except for', تاج شاهی = 'the crown of kingship' (تاج = crown, شاهی = kingship), چه = interrogative 'what', and افزایدت is an impersonal/3rd‑person-present-form with the 2nd‑person enclitic '-ت' meaning 'it adds to you' or 'will add you' (literary). So: 'What does it add to you, except the crown of kingship?'
segments.s598-g19: This is an imperative negative addressed to تو 'you': تو جان ... مده = 'Do not give your life'. از پی پادشاهی = 'for / in pursuit of kingship' (از پی = 'because of / in pursuit of', پادشاهی = 'kingdom/kingship'). Word order is subject-pronoun + object + prepositional phrase + negative imperative verb مده.
segments.s598-g20: This is a parallel admonition with the same negative imperative مده 'do not give'. خورش is used here poetically/metaphorically (literally 'a cooked dish' but in context meaning 'sustenance, comfort, or what one offers'); بی‌بهانه = 'without cause / unjustifiably'; به ماهی = 'to a ماهی' where ماهی in poetic usage can mean 'a beauty / a moonlike beloved' rather than a literal fish. So: 'do not give your sustenance/comfort without cause to a beauty.'
segments.s598-g21: همه بی‌گناهیم is a full predication 'we are all innocent' (بی‌گناه + first‑person plural copula -یم). و این کار تست is an independent clause: این کار 'this affair/matter' followed by تست — here تست is an archaic literary copular form (etymologically توست/تو است) meaning 'is yours' or 'is for you', so the clause reads 'and this affair is yours.' (Note: the provided sense list for تست does not match this archaic copula function.)
segments.s598-g22: جهان را is the definite object/topic 'the world' (را marks the definite object). همه دل 'all hearts' modifies or complements what follows; به بازار تست again uses تست as the archaic copula/possessive sense 'you have made / it is for you' and the phrase literally says you have put/placed 'all the hearts of the world in the marketplace' — i.e. you have exposed or put the world's hearts up for sale/for public view. Word order is topical (object first) and somewhat compressed/elliptical as is common in poetry.
segments.s598-g23: بدو گفت is an inverted phrase 'to him (بدو) he said' (classical word order); بهرام is the speaker's name. کای is a vocative particle ('O, Kay...' — Kay being a royal/title element), and دین‌پژوه is a vocative/noun phrase 'seeker/studier of religion' addressed to the addressee. Note that گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided sense list treated it as 'conversation', so that sense entry does not match the verb function).
segments.s598-g24: تو is the vocative/second‑person pronoun 'you'. زین here is an archaic contraction equivalent to از این 'from/for this' or 'because of this' (the listed senses for زین are not the demonstrative function, so none match exactly). بی‌گناهی = 'this innocence' (or 'our innocence'), و دیگر گروه = 'and the other group/company' — the segment is addressing the addressee in relation to 'this innocence and the other band', the construction is elliptical (poetic) and likely continues in the following lines.
segments.s598-g41: به یزدان پناهید is an optative/imperative construction: به + object (یزدان) names the locus of refuge and پناهید is a 2nd‑person (plural/polite) optative/imperative form meaning 'seek/take refuge'. The following کو begins a subordinate/relative clause ('for/who ...') and بد پناه is a nominative phrase ('a bad/poor protector') with adjective بد before the noun پناه.
segments.s598-g42: نماینده is an agentive noun 'one who shows/points out' (here 'guide/leader'); راه گم‌کرده راه should be read as the object/context of that showing: گم‌کرده is a past‑participle/adjectival phrase 'having lost (the) way' attached to/relating to راه, so the whole string describes 'one who shows the way to the one who has lost the way' (i.e. a guide for the way‑lost). Word order is poetic and compact: agent (نماینده) + object phrase (راه گم‌کرده راه).
segments.s598-g43: بشد is a past/perfect form of شدن used here as 'he rose/it came to be'; the subject خسرو follows the verb (typical poetic inversion). و links the clauses. برد here is used as a verbal past 'he carried/brought' and پیشش ('in his presence/before him') gives the direction; نماز is the direct object. The clause means 'Khosrow rose and brought the prayer before him.'
segments.s598-g44: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus (he) said' with such/چنین before the verb گفت. کای is the vocative particle 'O', addressing شاه; گردن‌فراز is an epithet placed after 'شاه' and means 'high‑necked/proud' (a poetic epithet). Together: 'thus he said, "O proud‑necked king,".'
segments.s598-g45: نشست is a noun 'sitting/session' (subject), تو 'your' marks possession; بر + گاه here means 'upon the seat/throne' (گاه used poetically for place/occasion) and فرخنده is the adjective 'auspicious' modifying گاه. باد is the optative particle 'may it be' placed at the end of the clause, yielding 'May your sitting on an auspicious throne be.'
segments.s598-g46: یلان جهان is a genitival phrase 'the heroes of the world' (ایلان = heroes, جهان = world). پیش تو 'in your presence/before you' gives the locus, and بنده باد is again optative 'may they be servants.' The word order places the possessor/world and location before the optative predicate.
segments.s598-g47: تو شاهی is a verbless nominal sentence 'you are king' (copula omitted, common in Persian). و links the clauses. ما بندگان توایم: ما is the subject 'we', بندگان 'servants/subjects', and توایم is a fixed predicative/possessive form 'are yours' (classical/poetic ending expressing belonging), so 'we are your servants.'
segments.s598-g48: به خوبی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for goodness' (به marking the domain), فزایندگان is a plural agent noun 'those who increase' or 'enhancers' and توایم repeats the predicative 'we are yours/with you'. Together the clause expresses the wish/identity 'may we be those who increase (your) goodness' or 'we are your increasers in goodness.'

sentences.s598-s33: Now I will tell a wondrous tale, from which no greater measure can be taken.
sentences.s598-s1: When Bahram and Khosrow came to the plain, they mounted a lion, with hearts full of blood.
sentences.s598-s2: When Khosrow saw those two living lions, one helmet lay placed in the midst.
sentences.s598-s3: He said to those mobeds: the crown is more fitting to me than to the first — for he sought kingship.
sentences.s598-s4: And another (reason) is that I am old and he is young — (he would be) weak in the claws of the living lion.
sentences.s598-s25: The great ones went forth and scattered jewels; upon that new crown they cried out 'Afarin.'
sentences.s598-s26: From the world there arose a cry throughout; in the ill-omened month Azar, on this festive day of Soroush.
sentences.s598-s27: A cloud arose and the moon grew dark; arrows rained from a black cloud.
sentences.s598-s28: I see neither sea nor plain nor longing; indeed, I see the air full of rooks.
sentences.s598-s29: The wind constantly scatters the harvests; what can be done by this lofty sky?
sentences.s598-s30: Nothing remained — no salted provisions, no firewood, no barley; nothing appears until a generous one (comes).
sentences.s598-s31: Because of this darkness of day and the fear of tribute, the earth has become, from the snow, like a mountain of ivory.
sentences.s598-s32: All the affairs lie in decline, unless Husayn Qetīb should take them in hand.
sentences.s598-s5: It is unfortunate that he should take the initiative, relying on youthful vigor and bodily strength.
sentences.s598-s6: Bahram said to him, 'Feigning deafness is permissible; we have no secret—speak plainly.'
sentences.s598-s7: A certain man, Gāvsar, lifted a mace; the whole world stood amazed at him.
sentences.s598-s8: The mobed said to him, 'O king—wise, learned, and pious.'
sentences.s598-s13: I am the rival of these male lions; I am the taker/acceptor of the battle of the brave.
sentences.s598-s14: The mobad said to him, 'Take refuge in God; when you go, wash your heart of sin.'
sentences.s598-s15: He did so as Bahram-shah had said; his heart became pure and he repented of sin.
sentences.s598-s16: He kept going with the cowherd's mace; when the quarrelsome lions saw (him)...
sentences.s598-s17: One of them quickly broke his chain and leapt upon the lofty king.
sentences.s598-s18: He struck him on the head with Bahram's mace, and the light left his eye.
sentences.s598-s19: Another came up and struck his head; blood poured down from his eye and from his wound.
sentences.s598-s20: The sovereign sat upon the ivory throne; he placed that heart-illumining crown upon his head.
sentences.s598-s9: What will that battle of lions you ordered add to you, apart from the crown of kingship?
sentences.s598-s10: Do not give your life in pursuit of kingship; do not give your sustenance (or comforts) without cause to a beauty.
sentences.s598-s11: We are all innocent, and this affair is yours; you have set the hearts of the whole world in the marketplace.
sentences.s598-s12: Bahram said to him, 'O Kay, scholar of religion: you — from this innocence and the other company...'
sentences.s598-s21: Seek refuge in God; for a poor protector is he who shows the lost the way.
sentences.s598-s22: Khosrow rose, and he brought the prayer before him; thus he said, 'O proud‑necked king,'
sentences.s598-s23: May your sitting on an auspicious throne be (blessed); may the heroes of the world be servants before you.
sentences.s598-s24: You are king, and we are your servants; may we be those who increase your goodness.
